summary: Mass-renamed many anonymous data labels that are used as PowerPC switch/jump-table bases from lbl_<address> to jtbl_<address>, and updated all assembly references plus the variables header comments to match. The change is semantic documentation only: incbin offsets/sizes and instruction bytes remain unchanged, but the label names now expose jump-table structure for future decompilation.

key_files.role: Renamed data-section labels from lbl_* to jtbl_* while preserving .global declarations, label addresses, incbin offsets, and incbin lengths.
Updated code references to the renamed jump-table symbols in lis/addi address materialization sites and related references.
Synced commented extern-style symbol inventory entries from lbl_* to jtbl_* for the identified jump tables.

what_changed: Changed numerous data labels such as lbl_803BAAA8, lbl_803BC99C, lbl_803BCC4C, and lbl_803BD0B0 to jtbl_803BAAA8, jtbl_803BC99C, jtbl_803BCC4C, and jtbl_803BD0B0.
Updated matching text references so address loads point at jtbl_* symbols, e.g. lis/addi pairs using lbl_803BAAA8@ha/@l became jtbl_803BAAA8@ha/@l.
Applied the same rename convention in include/variables.h comments, keeping the project-wide symbol list consistent with asm/data.s and asm/text.s.
No evidence in the diff of logic or binary data changes: the hunks rename symbols around existing instructions and .incbin directives.
smart_moves: The PR kept every renamed symbol address-stable by retaining the hexadecimal address suffix, making old lbl_* references easy to map to new jtbl_* names.
The renames were propagated through both the definition sites and all observed asm reference sites, avoiding stale labels that would break assembly or symbol lookup.
The author identified jump tables by their code-use pattern: bounds checks feeding scaled indexes into table loads, followed by mtctr for indirect branches.
Renaming labels before full C decompilation makes later switch reconstruction easier without needing to rediscover which anonymous data blocks are jump tables.
decomp_lessons: When an anonymous data blob is referenced through a scaled index and used with mtctr/bctr-style control flow, naming it jtbl_<address> is useful even before its cases are decompiled.
For large mechanical symbol-identification PRs, update the data definition, text references, and variables/header inventory in one pass to keep the repository searchable and buildable.
Preserve the original address in the symbol name when the semantic name is still generic; this keeps cross-references stable and reversible.
Semantic label cleanup can be valuable decomp groundwork even when no functions are matched or rewritten in C.
naming_conventions: Use jtbl_<ADDRESS> for identified jump tables, replacing generic lbl_<ADDRESS> labels.
Keep the hexadecimal address suffix unchanged and in the same style seen in the source, e.g. jtbl_803BAAA8.
Apply the jtbl_* name consistently to .global declarations, label definitions, @ha/@l relocations, and variables.h entries.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Recognize jump-table bases in PowerPC asm from sequences like lis/addi to a data label, slwi index by 2, lwzx from the table, then mtctr.
Look for preceding bounds checks such as cmplwi/cmpwi plus bgt/beq guarding the indexed dispatch.
Rename only the symbol around existing instructions and .incbin payloads when the goal is documentation/identification, not binary modification.

review_feedback.author: Cleverking2003
review_feedback.body: Cool, but I can't merge bc I don't have permissions
review_feedback.takeaway: The available review feedback was positive but contained no technical change requests.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that replace jtbl_* references with C switch statements.
Search for remaining lbl_* labels used in lis/addi + slwi + lwzx + mtctr patterns that may also be unidentified jump tables.
Check whether variables.h commented extern entries for jtbl_* symbols were later converted into typed declarations or removed after decompilation.
